blocks.content: Hundreds of configs exist but 20 cover 95% of real-world tuning problems. Memorize these — they appear in system design interviews and production debugging sessions constantly.

rows: Max size to auto-broadcast a join side
Fraction of executor heap for Spark memory
Fraction of unified memory reserved for cache
Max bytes per input partition when reading
Estimated cost of opening a file
Timeout for broadcast joins to complete
Scale executor count based on workload

blocks.content:
# ── Join optimization configs ──

# Raise broadcast threshold for better auto-broadcast decisions
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold', 100 * 1024 * 1024)  # 100 MB

# Give broadcasts more time (large clusters with high network latency)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.broadcastTimeout', 600)  # 10 minutes

# Preferred sort merge join block size (controls read buffer)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.sortMergeJoinExec.buffer.spillThreshold', '1g')

# Disable SMJ bucket cache to force re-reading (debug only)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.bucketing.enabled', 'true')  # enable bucketed joins

Goldilocks principle for partitions: Too few = not enough parallelism, large tasks, OOMs. Too many = scheduling overhead, tiny tasks, driver memory pressure. Target: 128-512 MB per partition, 2-4 tasks per CPU core.

# Partition sizing formula:
# ideal_partitions = total_data_size / target_partition_size
# Then: max(ideal, num_cpus * 2)

# Example: 1 TB data, 100 cores
total_gb = 1000
target_mb = 256
ideal = int(total_gb * 1024 / target_mb)  # → 4000 partitions
final = max(ideal, 100 * 2)  # → 4000 (use the larger)

spark.conf.set('spark.sql.shuffle.partitions', str(final))

# Input partition size (when reading files)
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes', 256 * 1024 * 1024)  # 256 MB

# Number of initial partitions when reading without explicit repartition
spark.conf.set('spark.default.parallelism', str(100 * 3))  # 3x cores

# Enable Kryo serializer — faster, smaller than Java default
spark.conf.set('spark.serializer', 'org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer')
spark.conf.set('spark.kryo.registrationRequired', 'false')  # auto-register
# Register custom classes for maximum Kryo perf:
spark.conf.set('spark.kryo.classesToRegister', 'com.mycompany.MyClass')

# Network timeouts — increase for large clusters or slow networks
spark.conf.set('spark.network.timeout', '400s')
spark.conf.set('spark.executor.heartbeatInterval', '30s')  # must be < network.timeout

# Shuffle compression (default: lz4 — fast)
spark.conf.set('spark.shuffle.compress', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.io.compression.codec', 'lz4')  # lz4 (fast) or zstd (better ratio)

rune_cards.definition: Number of partitions created after wide transformations (groupBy, join, distinct). Default 200. Too low = large partitions, OOM. Too high = tiny partitions, overhead. Rule: set to 2-4x number of CPU cores, or let AQE coalesce dynamically.

Alternative JVM serializer, 10x faster and produces 5x smaller objects than Java serialization. Enable: spark.serializer = org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer. Especially impactful for RDD operations and BroadcastHashJoin.

Spark scales executor count up/down based on pending task queue. spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true with YARN or Kubernetes. Idle executors are released; new executors requested when queue builds. Cost-efficient for variable workloads.

Number of tasks an executor can run concurrently. Default 1. Recommended: 4-5. Higher cores → better CPU utilization but more GC pressure per executor (more objects per heap). Rule: never exceed 5 cores per executor.

quiz.question: You're configuring a PySpark job for a 20-node cluster with 16 cores and 64 GB RAM each. What's a reasonable spark.sql.shuffle.partitions setting?
quiz.feedback: Total cluster cores = 320. Rule: 1-4x total cores. With AQE enabled, you can set 640 (2x) knowing AQE will coalesce small ones. Without AQE, 320 is safe. 200 is too low for 320 cores — many cores idle waiting. 10000 creates excessive overhead.

quiz.question: A job reads a 1 TB Parquet table. You see on the Spark UI that all 8000 input tasks are 128 KB each with 2ms duration. What config should you change?
options: Increase spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes from default 128 MB to 512 MB
quiz.feedback: 8000 tasks of 128 KB means Spark is creating one task per tiny file (or using a very small file split). Increasing maxPartitionBytes merges multiple small files into one partition → fewer, larger tasks → less scheduling overhead. This is the small file problem manifesting as excessive task count.
